data.title: Fortuna announces sale of non-core San Jose Mine, Mexico
Commodity Roundup: UBS slashes Brent forecast by $12/bbl as trade war weighs on oil prices
data.description:
U.S. tariffs and the trade war between the United States and China will likely weigh on economic growth this year and are likely to result in oil demand growing at a slower pace, UBS analysts said on Monday.

As such, the bank reduced its Brent forecasts by $12/bbl to $68-bbl, and expects WTI to trade at $64/bbl.

"There’s a high positive correlation between U.S. equities and crude oil prices at present, which usually indicates that demand factors are driving prices," Giovanni Staunovo, Strategist, UBS Switzerland AG said in a note.

Such a high correlation was seen during the global financial crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic.

"Some indicators, such as the futures curve being in backwardation (i.e., downward sloped), suggest that the oil market remains tight, but tariffs together with the ongoing uncertainty over theUS administration's next steps—that is, which tariffs are added next—are likely to weigh on economic growth."

Hence, UBS reduces it global oil demand growth forecast by 0.4mbpd to 0.8mbpd for 2025, as lower oil prices are also likely to reduce U.S. oil supply growth slightly this year.

Goldman Sachs meanwhile, expects oil prices to decline through the end of 2025 and into 2026, with Brent (CO1:COM) and WTI (CL1:COM) averaging $63 and $59 per barrel, respectively, this year and falling to $58 and $55 next year, due to rising recession risks and increased OPEC+ supply.

West Texas Intermediate (CL1:COM) crude futures were up +0.83% to $62.03 at the time of writing, while Brent crude (CO1:COM), the international benchmark, was +0.78% at $65.26 a barrel and has declined over 13% YTD.

Elsewhere, China’s commodity imports mostly fell year-on-year in March, with declines in coal (XAL1:COM), natural gas (NG1:COM), iron ore (SCO:COM), and soybean (S_1:COM).

Crude oil imports surged 20% to a 19-month-high, driven by record Iranian flows and stockpiling by private refiners due to concerns over US sanctions.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Monday: Tech stocks, and more
data.description:
Stock futures rose Monday morning after Trump’s new “reciprocal” tariffs exempted tech products like smartphones and chips, though officials warned the exemptions may not last.

Here are some of Monday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) +5.5% – Tech stocks saw a surge after the Trump administration temporarily excluded smartphones, laptops, and key electronics components from its new 145% reciprocal tariffs on Chinese goods. The U.S. Customs and Border Protection issued the exemptions late Friday, providing relief to tech stocks, though both President Trump and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick warned that these exemptions may not be permanent. Other notable gainers included Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) +3%, Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL) +7%, Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) +5.7%, Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE:HPE) +5%, and HP (NYSE:HPQ) +6%.
  • BP (NYSE:BP) +4% - Shares rose after the company announced the discovery of oil at the U.S. Far South prospect in the Gulf of Mexico. The discovery, which was made approximately 120 miles off the Louisiana coast at a depth of 23,830 feet, is part of BP’s strategy to increase its upstream production. Initial data suggests the presence of high-quality reservoirs and potentially commercial volumes of hydrocarbons. BP's revised strategy aims to increase its global upstream production to between 2.3M and 2.5M barrels of oil equivalent per day by 2030, with plans for further growth through 2035. The company also intends to drill around 40 wells over the next three years, with 10 to 15 wells expected to be drilled in 2025, underscoring its renewed focus on exploration.
  • Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) +2.5% – Shares rose after the company announced it would not sell Polish broadcaster TVN, putting an end to speculation surrounding the bidding process. In a statement on Monday, WBD explained that, following a strategic review of options for TVN, it had concluded that retaining ownership was the best path forward. The company emphasized its commitment to supporting its business and strategy, along with the journalistic efforts of its team. The decision comes amid financial market volatility triggered by U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariff policies, which have caused many investors to pause deal-making activities.
  • DuPont de Nemours (DD) +3% – Shares climbed after KeyBanc upgraded the stock to Overweight from Sector Weight, citing the company’s strong balance sheet as a key factor in its ability to navigate a potential economic downturn. Analyst Aleksey Yefremov highlighted DuPont’s electronics and water segments as "world-class secular growth franchises" that are currently trading at unusually discounted valuations.

data.title: Canadian, Danish pension funds rethink U.S. investments - report
data.description:
Top pension funds in Canada and Denmark are reassessing their U.S. investment strategy, as the world's largest economy is being viewed as too unstable in the backdrop of Donald Trump's erratic tariff policy, the Financial Times reported.

The Canada Pension Plan Investment Board is turning more cautious on its exposure to U.S. infrastructure, a person familiar with the fund's thinking told FT, amid fears of losing tax breaks. Another source said it would be "incredibly difficult" for CPPIB to commit new capital to U.S. private equity funds in the current geopolitical backdrop.

"Tariff noise makes it harder to evaluate businesses, and we have to take that into account until things settle down," said Martin Longchamps, head of private equity and credit at pension fund Caisse de dépôt et placement du Québec. But he added that the fund intends to stay active in the U.S.

A major Danish retirement fund halted new investments in U.S. private equity, and its executive told FT that the fund would resume investing "when things are more stable and foreseeable, or we will have to take a big discount."

An executive at another Danish fund - AkademikerPension - said he is considering "pretty fundamental changes" to his portfolio, which may lead to "significantly less strategic exposure to U.S. assets within a half year or so."

Stephanie Lose, Denmark's minister for economic affairs, said she's not aware of Danish funds changing their U.S. approach, but if they are, their decision "might be a side effect of both tariffs and Greenland."

data.title: Tech stocks rebound on Trump tariff exemptions
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) and other American tech stocks surged in premarket trading on Monday after the Trump administration temporarily exempted smartphones, computers and other electronics, mostly imported from China, from reciprocal tariffs.

The exclusions, published Friday by the U.S. Customs and Border Protection agency, follow Trump's moves to impose 145% tariffs on products from China and 10% baseline tariffs on almost every other nation.

The exemptions apply to popular electronics items such as smartphones, laptops, computers, computer processors, memory chips, flat panel TV displays, solar cells, hard drives.

Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) shares rose over 6.2% in early trade on Monday, while other U.S. tech stocks were also higher with Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) up nearly 3%, Dell Technologies (NYSE:DELL) +7%, Super Micro Computer (NASDAQ:SMCI) +5.7%, Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE:HPE) +5%, HP (NYSE:HPQ) +6% and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) +0.64%.

European semiconductor names, such as ASML Holding (NASDAQ:ASML) +2.4% and Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY), also gained.

KeyBanc analyst Brandon Nispel said that the tariff exemption is “probably the best case scenario” for Apple as it “takes a big risk off the table”, raising the recommendation on the stock to Sector Weight from Underweight.

However, Nispel added the stock is not fully “out of the woods” as “growth expectations remain high looking to FY26 and there is likely still consumer spending pullback occurring."

Seeking Alpha's Quant system rates Apple as Hold.

data.title: Ageas to buy UK-based esure from Bain Capital for £1.295B
Oil prices to fall through 2026 on supply glut – Goldman Sachs
data.description:
Goldman Sachs expects oil prices to decline through the end of 2025 and into 2026, with Brent (CO1:COM) and WTI (CL1:COM) averaging $63 and $59 per barrel, respectively, this year and falling to $58 and $55 next year, due to rising recession risks and increased OPEC+ supply.

The bank projects oil demand will increase by just 300,000 barrels per day between the end of 2024 and the end of 2025, amid a weak growth outlook driven by a global trade war.

Due to the escalating trade tensions between the U.S. and China, the bank has lowered its global oil demand growth forecast for the fourth quarter of 2026 by 900,000 barrels per day since mid-March. China on Friday announced an increase in tariffs on U.S. goods to 125%, up from the previous rate of 84%, effective April 12.

Goldman Sachs predicts that even with some future inventory builds already factored in, substantial surpluses of 800,000 barrels per day in 2025 and 1.4 million barrels per day in 2026 will keep oil prices under pressure.

West Texas Intermediate (CL1:COM) crude futures was up +0.16% to $61.60 at the time of writing, while Brent crude (CO1:COM), the international benchmark, was +0.06% at $64.80 a barrel and has declined over 13% YTD.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (LE)

data.title: CK Hutchison ports: Italian billionaire's business said to emerge as lead investor
data.description:
A port operator, majority owned by Italian billionaire Gianluigi Aponte's family-run shipping company, is emerging as the lead investor of the buyer group for CK Hutchison's (OTCPK:CKHUY) 43 ports, people familiar with the matter told Bloomberg. 

Terminal Investment Ltd ((TiL)) is expected to be the sole owner of all the ports, except for the two along the Panama Canal. TiL is 70% owned by Aponte's Mediterranean Shipping Co, 20% by BlackRock (NYSE:BLK) and 10% by Singapore's sovereign wealth fund.

BlackRock's (NYSE:BLK) unit Global Infrastructure Partners will likely own 51% of the two ports in Panama, while TiL would own the remaining stake. The two ports account for about 4% of the deal's total value. 

The consortium's structure was not explained when the deal was first announced last month. However, deliberations are still ongoing and the final structure could still change.

The deal faces an uphill regulatory battle, as the Chinese government is unhappy with the agreement and Panama's top auditor has accused CK Hutchison (OTCPK:CKHUY) of unpaid fees.

data.title: Europe indexes start week with strong gains after Trump tariff exemptions on tech
data.description:
London (UKX) +1.92% to 8,117. 

Germany (DAX:IND) +2.29% to 20,834.

France (CAC:IND) +2.11% to 7,254.

Switzerland’s producer and import prices edged down by 0.1 Y/Y in March.

Hungary's construction output declined by 4.5% Y/Y in February.

The annual inflation rate in Finland held steady at 0.5% in March.

The Netherlands’ trade surplus narrowed to €10.92B in February.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) edged 2.04% higher to 496.98, with all sectors in green, bolstered by technology gains, after Trump announced temporary tariff exemptions on smartphones, computers, and other electronics primarily imported from China. Meanwhile, investor focus is also turning to EU-US trade talks, as EU Trade Commissioner Maros Sefcovic is reportedly set to meet with U.S. officials.

Top gainers for the day include Deutsche Bank (NYSE:DB), Infineon (OTCQX:IFNNY), Sartorius (OTCPK:SARTF), and Heidelberg Materials (OTCPK:HDLMY). 

Coming up in the session: A lighter day on the economic calendar today.

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was down 3 basis points to 4.47%.

Germany's 10-year yield was up 2 basis points to 2.55%.

UK's 10-year yield was down 4 basis points to 4.71%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)
